serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of marinated jellyfish is approximately 100 g, often presented as a cold appetiser or side dish in a small bowl or plate.

nutrient_source.title: Marinated Jellyfish — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F03060017 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this source. dietary_fibre_g is reported as -1 in the source, indicating the value was not measured or reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson, Foster-Powell & Brand-Miller
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal); GI data anchored to University of Sydney GI Database methodology
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for marinated jellyfish. Jellyfish itself contains negligible intrinsic carbohydrate; the carbohydrate content of 8.53 g/100g derives primarily from the marinade (sugar, vinegar). The GI of the marinade sugar component is estimated using sucrose GI = 65 (University of Sydney GI Database, sucrose reference) diluted by the low-GI vinegar and sesame components. A blended dish GI of approximately 52 (low range) is applied, consistent with the analogous cold dressed seafood salad with sugar-vinegar marinade category. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (52 × 8.53) / 100 = 4.4 g. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 316 mg/100g from soy sauce marinade and residual salt from desalination processing
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit marinated jellyfish to small portions (50 g or less) and request reduced-soy-sauce preparation, as the 316 mg sodium per 100 g contributes approximately 14% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit in a single 100 g serving; thorough desalination soaking before marinating is critical to reduce residual sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar 8.32 g/100g from marinade with dietary fibre not reported (likely negligible), yielding a low glycemic load but non-trivial sugar exposure
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume marinated jellyfish in modest portions (50–80 g) given the 8.32 g added sugar per 100 g from the marinade; the estimated low GI (~52) and low glycemic load (~4.4 per 100 g) are favourable, but the absence of dietary fibre means there is no fibre buffer to blunt glucose absorption, and the vinegar component provides only modest glucose-lowering benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 316 mg/100g and added sugar 8.32 g/100g; low total fat 1.46 g/100g is favourable but sodium and sugar load require attention
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, marinated jellyfish is low in total fat (1.46 g/100g) and calories (66 kcal/100g), which is favourable, but the 316 mg sodium per 100 g and 8.32 g added sugar per 100 g are clinically relevant; portion size should be kept to 50–80 g and preparation with reduced soy sauce and sugar is recommended to align with heart-healthy dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar 8.32 g/100g and sodium 316 mg/100g may promote inflammation; red chilli (nightshade) may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should exercise caution with marinated jellyfish due to the 8.32 g added sugar and 316 mg sodium per 100 g, both of which can promote pro-inflammatory pathways; red chilli is a nightshade ingredient that may aggravate symptoms in solanine-sensitive patients, and reduced-sugar, reduced-soy-sauce preparation is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Marinated jellyfish (凉拌海蜇, Liang Ban Hai Zhe) is a cold seafood appetiser popular in Singaporean Chinese cuisine and across Chinese-speaking communities in Southeast Asia. It is prepared from processed jellyfish that has been desalinated through repeated soaking in fresh water, then dressed in a marinade typically comprising soy sauce, rice vinegar, sesame oil, sesame seeds, red chilli, garlic, and sugar. The dish is served cold or at room temperature and is prized for its distinctive crunchy, gelatinous texture and its savoury-sweet-tangy flavour profile. Nutritionally, marinated jellyfish is low in calories (66 kcal/100g) and total fat (1.46 g/100g), with a moderate protein content of 4.63 g/100g derived from the jellyfish collagen matrix. The carbohydrate content of 8.53 g/100g and sugar content of 8.32 g/100g arise almost entirely from the marinade rather than the jellyfish itself. Sodium at 316 mg/100g reflects both the soy sauce marinade and residual salt from the desalination process; thorough soaking is essential to reduce sodium to safe levels. Dietary fibre data was not reported in the HPB source. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to added sugar without a fibre buffer. The sesame seeds contribute small amounts of calcium (6 mg/100g) and iron (1.26 mg/100g). The dish is commonly served as a starter or side dish at Chinese banquets, hawker centres, and zi char restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F03060017, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Source match is exact. Multilingual names are well-established for this dish in Singaporean Chinese culinary tradition. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence medium for GI component only); overall record confidence is high given the exact HPB source match for all nutrient values.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is set to -1: the HPB Singapore Foodo source (cr_id F03060017) reports dietary fibre as -1, indicating the value was not measured or reported for this preparation. Limited published fibre data exists for marinated jellyfish in USDA FoodData Central or the HPB database as of 2026. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for marinated jellyfish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. The input record carried a dietary_tag of 'plant_based' which has been removed as jellyfish is an animal-derived product. The input GI value of 0 with confidence 0.3 from 'Embedded GI Database' has been replaced with an analogous estimate of GI 52 (low) derived from peer-reviewed GI literature, as the original source is not an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2 policy. Halal status is marked false as jellyfish is considered non-halal under most mainstream Islamic jurisprudence (Shafi'i school prevalent in Singapore); consumers should verify with their religious authority.
